replies.author: @NEOs_Hyper
replies.text:
​@norabarnes9235 Nothing in existence can be perfect. From a physical, mathematical, and philosophical standpoint, nothing in the real, existing world can be considered truly perfect. Therefore in order to exist in the real world as a program you have to accept imperfection/the meaning of being human. As an ISO Qourra was already imperfect. The concept of perfection typically belongs to the realm of ideals and abstractions rather than reality. Maybe in the digital world but even then. That was the point of the conflict with Clu. Kevin Flynn said that chasing perfection was a mistake and that true value is found in the imperfect and the unexpected. The permanence code was a piece of software written by Kevin Flynn decades earlier and is the MacGuffin of the Ares plot. 
The 29-minute barrier: In Ares, digital programs can only survive in the real world for 29 minutes before they "derez" and return to the Grid. By the way this problem would've also affected CLU, but with the Master Key he likely would've had the power to come back over and over on his own. The permanence code allows a program to overcome this barrier and exist indefinitely.
A different kind of permanence: The film clarifies that Quorra's unique, ISO-derived ability to exist in the real world is different from the code used by the program Ares. Quorra did not need the permanence code because she is an Isomorphic Algorithm (ISO), a unique type of program that is fundamentally different from a standard program like CLU or Ares. Therefore, Ares's story does not contradict what is shown with Quorra in Legacy. And what I'm saying also doesn't contradict the ending of Legacy either if you really understand what happened at the end when she goes to the real world with sam. The process of entering the real world created a new, physical body for Quorra with human DNA. This means she is no longer a purely digital being that exists only as code. Since Quorra became a human being at the end of Tron: Legacy, she is subject to all human biology and its limitations, which means she could get cancer. The film's narrative establishes that once she transitioned from an Isomorphic Algorithm (ISO) to a human, she is physically and genetically human. In summary, for Quorra to truly be human and live in the real world, she must be susceptible to all the challenges of human biology, including disease. Her origin as an ISO is significant for her personality and unique abilities, but it doesn't provide a shield from biological imperfections such as developing cancer. Remember there's a fundamental difference between an ISO and a program. Programs require the Permanence Code. ISOs have a natural sort of permanence code since they were never written to begin with which is why they can't be simply be deleted with "one keystroke." Also, Julian Dillinger's mother is Elisabeth Dillinger. Making Julian Dillinger the nephew of Edward Dillinger and the Grandson of the late Ed Dillinger Sr. They did do a good job. Sometimes you just have to do a bit of research. Just because characters aren't introduced in a movie doesn't mean they don't exist in the background.

replies.text: @Saxgodits not a great film. you have a right to your opinion, but i disagree with you. The movie completely cut out Sam in a weird way, gave the entire company to two asian women, no blood relation, the second CPU is a black woman and the ONLY black person in the film, and she was given a bizarre death scene, they really highlighted her, there only black person. So tight off the bat, you have forced diversity, but given the lack of it in actuality, its all so fake and transparent. THEN you have things like the Tron world coming to us. The ads really built it up and I thought there was gonna be a take over but instead you have disjointed starts and stops--and yet, the death toll and physical destruction went VERY high. Where as Legacy you got to be in the world one long stretch and really get a feel. It had aspects that were cool like watching what hacking looks like from the perspective, or the 80s tron world. But overall it lacked a sense of cohesion, like we were teased with separate little pieces. And i was left unsure in the end, because Ares had the coords which excited me but then he said "maybe eventually i will choose to check it out" and Im left unsure if its a set up for a baseless tease. Leto was okay as a learning AI and I really appreciated he didnt hate humanity, thats never done. But it was nowhere near as good as legacy. The CPUs couldnt even decide if they were CPU or human. Sometimes they did the weird robot movements like Legacy, sometimes they walked and seemed human. Also the lighting was off a lot, especially one memorable time over exposed with Letos face. There were cool moments, like watching the lighting was off bird things make ribbons everywhere, but the good parts couldnt make up for the bad.

CLU upset he was abandoned by his creator (an allegory to an absent parent). Sam coming to terms that his father actually didn't abandon him and the emotions that go with that. author: @ynrikotowers4374
text:
The movie was good — not perfect, but still good. It could’ve been great, even a cult classic, if the story was done better.

Dillinger was a solid villain, especially since he contrasts nicely with Kevin Flynn — Kevin being wiser and older, while Dillinger felt impulsive and childish. The mom’s character could’ve been better though; instead of just criticizing, she could’ve offered actual guidance or something meaningful to do.

I really liked the concept of Programs having limited time in the real world, and the idea of the permanence code was great too. It would’ve been even better if it tied into ISO coding and Eve’s story. Imagine if her main goal was to resurrect her sister — something anyone grieving would want. It could’ve shown her journey through grief: from obsession with bringing her sister back to finally learning to let go.

Ares could’ve been a rogue super soldier, not a superhero — someone who just wanted to live, doing whatever it takes to survive. That kind of flawed drive would’ve made his arc way more human. Dillinger, on the other hand, could’ve learned that control in the real world isn’t as simple as fixing bugs or patching systems.

The climax could’ve been amazing: Eve finally brings her sister back, but the copy calls herself Athena — not really her sister anymore. Ares and Athena fight in the rain, with Ares getting overwhelmed. Eve helps him by shooting Athena with the laser gun — the one that digitizes, not kills — and finally accepts that her sister is gone. Athena escapes the Encom tower, while Dillinger gets cornered by police. Then the workshop activates on its own, digitizing him into the system. He meets Athena again and takes on the mantle of Master Control.

They could’ve even explained that the transfer from the Grid to the real world happens through information-to-matter conversion, which would’ve tied everything together perfectly.

Same characters, same themes — just way more flavor and meaning. I wish the movie went this route instead of ending on a cliffhanger no one really wanted.

text: Just finished watching this.   Cheap ticket night at our favorite theater.   We were 4 of 8 in the theater.   I like the soundtrack a bit.  It’s a depressing storyline, to what could have been an epic tale.   I felt the story was being told by a different story teller, from a different perspective.  The first films were from an Encom viewpoint, or Flynn, and this seemed like a completely separate world within the ecosphere.   The grid separation was to me the key, the concept of these different grids not directly connected but existing independently and evolving as such.   The characters weren’t terrible just rushed.   This could have been so much better.   I hate to admit there were elements I liked.   I think if they had expanded those parts of the world  it would have been more of an epic release and update to the franchise.   Missed opportunity.   5 out of 10

replies.text:
I watched it in 3D, some of the effects were cool as hell, like the giant CGI face in the grid.  Yet, the characters were FLAT.  Ares had no arc.  He had no real motivation to disobey orders.  It was thin as Swiss cheese.  Letto acted exactly the same throughout the entire film.  

Last night I decided to pull out the original Tron for a comparative rewatch right after seeing Ares.  What i drew from that experience was this, Tron’s story was thin.  But it made up for it with characters that were endearing and just fantastic performances by the cast.
.
Ares had a thin plot also but it had characters and acting that frankly I couldn’t care less about.  Almost No One was likable in Ares.  No one.

I didn’t hate Ares but at least Legacy had some characters that you sorta liked and it had some great performances in it.

This movie deserves a shot, it's the only Disney thing I've fully watched since Endgame, cuz the rest was garbage. I got chills like damn near a dozen times. That soundtrack was phenomenal; it ain't Daft Punk, but it was right behind them and sounded like the went full tilt for the Tron vibe while adding a serious edge to it.

Do I wish it had Sam and Quorra? Sure! But was it awful? Absolutely not. I was engaged the entire time. Even Leto was enjoyable, actually endearing at times.
